nativeRange.habitat: The Volcano Killifish is native to crystal-clear, fast-moving outflows of limestone karst springs and small hillside streams on Muna Island, Sulawesi, Indonesia. The substrate in these habitats consists of sand, gravel, and leaf litter with submerged vegetation. The underlying limestone geology produces water that is hard, distinctly alkaline, and highly oxygenated.

decorPreferences: fine-leaved floating plants (Salvinia, Riccia) for egg-laying and surface cover
clumps of Java moss or submerged mosses as spawning sites
gentle filtration (sponge filter) to maintain oxygenation without strong current

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild; prefers very small live and frozen foods such as micro worms, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), daphnia, and cyclops. Will accept high-quality micro-pellets and crushed flake once acclimated. Food particle size should not exceed ~1 mm given the fish’s small mouth. A varied diet with regular live or frozen offerings brings out the best coloration in males.

goodWith: conspecifics in species-only or biotope setups
small freshwater shrimp (Caridina, Neocaridina) in species tanks — adults generally ignored
avoidWith: larger fish that may eat or harass them
fish requiring soft, acidic water (incompatible water chemistry)
fast, competitive feeders that outcompete them for micro-foods

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by The Last 99; 1024x674. Sharp group shot of four fish showing electric-blue iridescent flanks and red-margined fins — best represents this shoaling species.
Photo by Aquakeeper14; 640x480. Single male showing the vivid red-orange body coloration; slightly soft focus and lower resolution, kept as a colour reference.

summary: The Volcano Killifish (Oryzias woworae), also sold as Daisy's Ricefish, is a tiny, brilliantly coloured ricefish from karst springs on Muna Island, Sulawesi. Males display vivid red-orange bodies with blue iridescent fins. Hard, alkaline water and micro-sized foods are essential.
careNotes: Oryzias woworae was formally described in 2010 by Parenti & Hadiaty from Muna Island, Sulawesi, Indonesia — one of the most recently described and visually striking members of the ricefish family Adrianichthyidae. Males are vividly coloured: the body is suffused with intense red-orange, and the fins and flanks carry electric-blue iridescence. This degree of colour difference between the sexes — where males and females look strikingly unlike one another — is rarely seen in fish of this size class. Females are considerably plainer, showing olive-grey tones. Adults reach only about 3 cm, making them one of the smallest aquarium fish. The species’ wild habitat is the crystal-clear, highly oxygenated outflows of limestone (karst) springs on Muna Island. This geology produces water that is hard and distinctly alkaline (pH 7.5–8.5, gH 10–20) — the opposite of most Southeast Asian fish. Replicating this chemistry is the single most critical husbandry requirement: soft or acidic water causes chronic stress and early mortality. Crushed coral, aragonite substrate, or limestone rocks can be used to maintain hardness and buffer pH in soft-water areas. Temperature should be kept in the 24–28°C band; lower temperatures are acceptable short-term but suppress breeding activity and colour. A tight-fitting lid is essential as Oryzias are accomplished jumpers. The species is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List due to its extremely restricted endemic range (a single island), ongoing habitat degradation from agriculture, and collection pressure. Hobbyists are encouraged to source tank-raised stock and to maintain responsible captive populations. Intermediate care is warranted not due to aggression or feeding difficulty, but because of the atypical water chemistry requirements and the need for appropriately sized micro-foods.
breedingNotes: Oryzias woworae is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or moss. Conditioning pairs with live micro worms and baby brine shrimp triggers spawning readily; males display intensely toward females. Eggs (typically 5–15 per spawning event) are deposited daily and may be carried briefly attached to the female’s vent before being deposited on plant material. Eggs hatch in approximately 10–14 days at 26°C. Adults will eat eggs and fry if given the opportunity; a dedicated breeding tank with spawning mops or dense moss and daily egg removal to a separate rearing vessel is recommended. Fry are very small on hatching and require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods for the first week before graduating to freshly hatched Artemia nauplii. Breedability is rated moderate as the micro-scale fry demands careful husbandry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Oryzias woworae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided habitat (Muna Island karst springs), water parameters (temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 10–20 dH), adult size (~3 cm), diet, breeding behaviour, tank setup, and conservation notes. Original description: Parenti & Hadiaty (2010).

additional.name: FishBase — Oryzias woworae
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Adrianichthyidae, Beloniformes), native range (Muna Island, Sulawesi, Indonesia), adult size (to 3.5 cm TL), temperature range, and IUCN status (Vulnerable). Confirmed 2010 Parenti & Hadiaty description.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Oryzias woworae care guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-focused care guide covering tank size, water chemistry (hard alkaline), micro-food requirements, group size recommendation, and breeding tips for this species. Trade name Daisy’s Ricefish confirmed.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Oryzias woworae profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming Sulawesi karst-spring biotope, red-blue male dichromatism, small adult size, and alkaline water requirements. IUCN Vulnerable status and restricted range noted.

fish.breedingNotes: The Volcano Killifish is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs among fine plant material or spawning mops on a daily basis. Because adults readily eat their own eggs, moving eggs to a separate rearing vessel each day is strongly recommended. Fry are very small at hatching and require infusoria or prepared fry foods for the first week before graduating to freshly hatched Artemia nauplii. Conditioning adults with live foods tends to trigger spawning readily.
